title: Clearing a blocked toilet
subtitle: Unblock a slow-flush or fully blocked pan using a plunger and drain snake
excerpt: How to clear a blocked toilet: confirming the blockage location, using a flange plunger correctly to generate pressure in the trap, using a drain snake for blockages a plunger cannot shift, and knowing when the blockage is beyond the pan and needs a plumber.

content.text: Wear rubber gloves before starting. Most toilet blockages are in the
. Do not use caustic or chemical drain cleaners in a toilet: they generate heat, can splash back, and attack the rubber seal in the cistern inlet valve.

If the water in the pan rises but does not overflow, the blockage is in the pan trap or the short pipe to the soil stack. This is the common home case. If the water rises in the pan and at the same time backs up through floor gullies or other fixtures, the blockage is in the shared drain outside the house. That is not a DIY job.

Ensure there is enough water in the pan to cover the plunger cup; add water from a bucket if needed. Insert the flange plunger into the pan outlet and press firmly to expel air and seat the flange in the outlet. Plunge with firm, rhythmic strokes, pushing down and then pulling back sharply. The back-stroke is as important as the forward stroke; pulling back creates negative pressure that dislodges soft blockages. Continue for 10 to 15 strokes.

After plunging, add a bucket of water to the pan slowly to test whether the outlet is clear before flushing the cistern. If the water drains away, the blockage is cleared. Flush once to confirm normal operation. If the water rises again, repeat the plunging sequence before trying the drain snake.
Step 4, use a drain snake if plunging fails

Feed a 5 m (15 ft) drain snake slowly into the pan outlet by cranking the handle clockwise. When it meets resistance, crank steadily while pushing forward; do not force it aggressively as the pan ceramic can crack. Once through the blockage, crank in reverse to hook any solid material, then withdraw and clean the snake before re-feeding. Repeat until the snake passes freely.

Call a plumber if any of these happen. The blockage does not clear after two full plunging rounds and a drain snake pass. Water backs up through other fittings in the house. The toilet blocks again and again over a short stretch of time. Repeat blockages point to a partial collapse or roots in the outside drain, which needs a CCTV survey.

attrs.fix: Use a flange plunger. If a cup plunger is all that is available, fold the flange up into the cup before inserting it; this gives a partial seal on the outlet curve.
attrs.cause: The cup plunger is being used instead of a flange plunger; the cup cannot seal against the curved toilet outlet.
attrs.symptom: Plunger squirts water sideways and does not feel like it is generating pressure

attrs.fix: Pour a full bucket of hot (not boiling) water from waist height to flush the waste run. If slow flushing continues, a plumber's CCTV survey will locate the partial restriction.
attrs.cause: A partial blockage remains further down the waste run, or the internal trap has rough or damaged surfaces collecting debris.
attrs.symptom: Pan empties slowly after clearing but not at full rate
